title: Podman not able to start pod from jenkins
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

Hi all, let me describe better what happened. Currently I have a rootless user for podman called myuserpod. In addition to that I have on the same machine also a jenkins user that have some sudo privileges to launch podman command as myuserpod. 
jenkins ALL=(myuserpod) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/podman
What happened now is that, launching a pod using the command:
#podman play kube pod_mypod.yaml --> directly from myuserpod shell work fine 
#sudo -u myuserpod /usr/bin/podman play kube pod_mypod.yaml --> from jenkins pipeline start the pod but it ends in error with the following sentences:
- /bin/sh: 1: chmod: Transport endpoint is not connected
- /bin/sh: 1: ./startmyscript.sh: Transport endpoint is not connected

here below the contents of pod_mypod.yaml
######################################################
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  labels:
    app: my-pod
  name: my-pod
spec:
  restartPolicy: Never

  containers:
    - name: my_container
      image: localhost/myimage:1.0.2.6
      tty: true
      volumeMounts:
        - name: bigstorage
          mountPath: /Big-Storage
      ports:
        - containerPort: 1521
          hostPort: 1597
        - containerPort: 10443
          hostPort: 10999
        - containerPort: 12443
          hostPort: 12998
      command: [ "/bin/sh", "-c" ]
      args:
        - echo starting;
          ulimit -a;
          ls -latr ./startmyscript.sh;
          chmod +x ./startmyscript.sh;
          ./startmyscript.sh;

  volumes:
    - name: bigstorage
      persistentVolumeClaim:
        claimName: bigstorage

######################################################


**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Launch the command sudo -u myuserpod /usr/bin/podman play kube pod_mypod.yaml from jenkins pipeline 

2.

3.

**Describe the results you received:**
- /bin/sh: 1: chmod: Transport endpoint is not connected
- /bin/sh: 1: ./startmyscript.sh: Transport endpoint is not connected

**Describe the results you expected:**
Everything works fine. Pod and container should run without errors

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 3.0.2-dev
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

  arch: amd64
  buildahVersion: 1.19.8
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md)**


YES

comments.body:
I discovered something new. If I add a sleep 3m at the end of jenkins pipeline, the pod work fine during all the 3minutes. 
This means that some processes died together with the jenkins bash launching the podman commands invalidating the pod status. Is there any possible solution to this behaviour?

The first bit that is suspicious to me is the `sudo -u`. Podman requires a login session be available for the user we are run as, and sudo usually does not do this. You can try doing a `loginctl enable-linger` for the user you are running Podman as.

Hi mheon, many thanks for your feedback.
I've tried to launch the command: "loginctl enable-linger jenkins"
In addition to that I've removed the sudo on the second user and configured the user jenkins as podman user as well in order to try to understand the core issue.
Unfortunately the issue remain. Until the jenkins pipeline is running the pod started and works well. As soon as it ends the pod lose all the references related to filesystem (it is not able to find any path or directory and show the error Transport endpoint is not connected). 
If later, using always the jenkins user, I stop and restart the pod, everything works fine again.
I do not know why but the end of the jenkins bash pipeline where the pod is started put something in bad status on the respect of the filesystem.

I tried also to see if some process ends after the end of the jenkins pipeline bash, but none do.
Does the command "loginctl enable-linger jenkins" need a reboot to work?

No.

Is the Jenkins pipeline doing anything with the filesystem - NFS mounts, maybe?

The yaml instead start from a local image that make some activities like:


RUN mkdir -p /Local-Storage/${IMAGE}/${VERSION}
COPY ./package/${IMAGE}-${VERSION}.tar.gz /Local-Storage/${IMAGE}/${VERSION}/
COPY ./cfg/online/igfs.properties /Local-Storage/${IMAGE}/${VERSION}/
WORKDIR /Local-Storage/${IMAGE}/${VERSION}
RUN tar -xvzf ${IMAGE}-${VERSION}.tar.gz
RUN cp igfs.properties cfg/online/igfs.properties
since the user `myuserpod` is used to launch containers, don't you need something like `loginctl enable-linger myuserpod` instead of using the `jenkins` user?
hi giuseppe, after the suggest of mheon i removed the intermediate layer of myuserpod, using only jenkins user to execute podman commands too.

can you verify if there is the user systemd session running at any time? Even after the jenkins user logs out?

Could it be jenkins to kill all its processes?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since no feedback in a month, I figure @pyronaid has fixed his issue. CLosing.

Just to add a "happy end" here:

I had the same issue, and the problem is that when a Jenkins job finishes, it makes sure that any process started during that job also gets killed. This is the default behavior.

If you want to circumvent that, you can set an env var (BUILD_ID) just before starting the desired process.
Ex.:
`BUILD_ID=dontKillMe podman run -d ...`

This is also described here:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/39169457/how-to-tell-jenkins-not-to-kill-processes-after-successful-execution-of-job-in-m
